query: What demand charges apply to commercial customers with demand >500 kW in California, within PG&E's territory, based on the OpenEI tariffs data? Answer should contain service class name and demand charge for at least 2 classes
final_answer:
Now I have a comprehensive view. Here is the complete answer:

---

## PG&E Commercial Demand Charges for Customers with Demand >500 kW

Based on the OpenEI IURDB (Pacific Gas & Electric Co, EIA ID: 14328), the currently active commercial tariffs that apply to customers with demand exceeding 500 kW are the **E-19 Medium General Demand TOU** schedules (for 500–999 kW) and the newer **B-19** successor. The database returns the current active E-19 tariffs (effective March 27, 2026). The E-20/B-20 schedules (for ≥1,000 kW) are also relevant but are not in the returned active set with full demand detail in this data pull.

Below are **three distinct service classes** with their demand charges:

---

### 1. E-19 Medium General Demand TOU (Secondary) — 500–999 kW

**Source:** OpenEI rate label `69de7e4cd600f18ec30600ac`, effective March 27, 2026. PG&E Tariff Book Schedule E-19.

**Demand charges are TOU-based** with both a flat (customer) demand charge and TOU-period demand charges:

| Charge Component | Winter (Nov–Apr) | Summer (May–Oct) |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat demand charge** | $0/kW | **$42.10/kW** |
| **TOU – Off-peak** (hours 0–7, weekends) | $0/kW | $0/kW |
| **TOU – Partial-peak** (hours 8–11, 19–21 weekdays) | $0/kW | **$16.44/kW** |
| **TOU – Peak** (hours 12–18 weekdays) | $0/kW | **$21.59/kW** |

*Measurement:* Demand is the highest 15-minute average in the billing month.

---

### 2. E-19 Medium General Demand TOU Option R (Primary) — 500–999 kW

**Source:** OpenEI rate label `69de7c756d1ebb0263094f0d`, effective March 27, 2026.

Primary voltage service (lower losses) gets lower demand charges:

| Charge Component | Winter (Nov–Apr) | Summer (May–Oct) |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat demand charge** | $0/kW | **$33.10/kW** |
| **TOU – Off-peak** | $0/kW | $0/kW |
| **TOU – Partial-peak** | $0/kW | **$14.86/kW** |
| **TOU – Peak** | $0/kW | **$18.71/kW** |

---

### 3. E-19 Medium General Demand TOU Option R (Transmission) — 500–999 kW

**Source:** OpenEI rate label `69de7d02edea9919e60bad8e`, effective March 27, 2026.

Transmission-level service has the simplest structure — a single flat demand charge year-round:

| Charge Component | All Year |
|---|---|
| **Flat demand charge** | **$16.47/kW** |
| **TOU demand charges** | $0/kW (all periods) |

---

### Additional Context

- **E-19 (Secondary, mandatory)** — for customers whose demand has exceeded **499 kW** for at least 3 consecutive months. Per the PG&E tariff description: *"A customer must take service under Schedule E-19 if the customer's maximum billing demand has exceeded 499 kilowatts for at least three consecutive months."*

- **For customers with demand ≥1,000 kW**, PG&E's **E-20 / B-20** schedules apply (mandatory transition from legacy E-20 to B-20 began March 2021). The OpenEI historical E-20 data showed demand rate structures such as $11.98/kW (partial-peak) and $16.37/kW (peak) on secondary, and $3.26/kW and $15.03/kW on transmission-level service (2014 vintage rates, now superseded by B-20).

- **B-19 / B-20** are the current replacement schedules with revised TOU periods (4:00–9:00 PM peak instead of legacy hours). Per PG&E's published rate plan overview (pge.com), B-19 serves 500–999 kW and B-20 serves ≥1,000 kW, both with demand charges — but the full B-19/B-20 rate details were not available in the OpenEI query results at this time.

---

**Bottom line:** For a commercial customer with >500 kW demand in PG&E territory, the applicable demand charges depend on voltage level. On **secondary** voltage (most common), the summer demand charge totals $42.10/kW (flat) + $16.44/kW (partial-peak) + $21.59/kW (peak) = effectively **$80.13/kW** in the peak TOU period during summer, though typically only the maximum demand in each TOU period is billed. On **primary** voltage, the total summer peak demand charge is $33.10 + $18.71 = **$51.81/kW**. On **transmission** voltage, it's a flat **$16.47/kW** year-round with no TOU adder.
